title: Chapter 9: Enter the King
content: The armored SUV rolled into Parking Garage B1 like a middle finger to the apocalypse.

It was absurd—a matte-black tank of a vehicle, headlights cutting through the garage's perpetual dusk, engine purring at a volume barely above a whisper, rolling past the rusted corpses of sedans and minivans that would never move again. It looked like a billionaire's escape pod, and Marcus—behind the wheel for the first time in two years, fingers white-knuckled on leather that probably cost more than his rent—felt like an imposter driving someone else's car.
Which he was. The System's car. The System's everything.
But the look on Derek Cole's face was *priceless*.

Derek was sitting on the SUV's hood—their old SUV, the broken one with the dead battery—when the armored vehicle rounded the corner. He had a protein bar in one hand and the crowbar in the other, and for one perfect, frozen moment, his expression cycled through confusion, disbelief, and naked greed before settling on hostility.
Elena was beside him, closer to the concrete wall than to the car, and her reaction was different: her eyes went wide, then narrowed, then landed on something that looked a lot like hope.
Marcus parked. The engine died with a whisper. He stepped out.
"Morning," he said.
Derek's crowbar twitched. "The hell is this?"

"Breakfast." Marcus opened the back door and retrieved the gourmet meal kit—six containers arranged in a thermal bag that kept everything warm. The smell hit the garage like a grenade: seared salmon, rosemary, garlic, fresh bread. Scents that didn't exist anymore, layered over oil and rust and death.
Elena's nostrils flared. Her hand went to her stomach—an involuntary, almost desperate gesture.
Derek smelled it too. His jaw tightened. The greed warred with hostility on his face, and for a moment, greed won. "Where did you get that?"
"Does it matter?" Marcus set the bag on the hood of the armored SUV. "There's enough for everyone. Six servings."
"How?"

"System." Marcus shrugged. "Long story. Short version: I have resources. You don't. I have a fortress. You have a parking space. I have—" He gestured to Sera, who emerged from the shadows of the garage entrance, Shadow Weave catching the low light like oil on water. "—the deadliest companion in the city. You have a crowbar."
Derek's hand tightened on the crowbar until his knuckles went white.

Elena, though—Elena was looking at Sera with an expression Marcus couldn't quite decode. Not fear. Not disgust. Something more complicated. *Recognition*, maybe—the recognition of someone who'd been through hell and come out the other side wearing it like armor.
"You're the zombie," Elena said to Sera. "From yesterday. You can talk."

"I can do more than talk." Sera's voice was calm, measured. "But right now, I'm choosing to stand here and be civil, because Marcus asked me to. Because he believes—" A flicker of something in her grey eyes. "—that everyone deserves a choice."
"Choice." Derek spat the word. "What choice? Eat your charity and owe you forever?"

"No owing." Marcus popped open the first container. The salmon glistened, pink and perfect, next to a bed of vegetables that looked like they'd been harvested five minutes ago. "Eat or don't. Stay or go. I'm not keeping anyone."
He took a piece of bread, tore off a chunk, and ate it. Casual. Easy. Like the apocalypse hadn't happened and fresh bread was something you just *had*.

Elena watched him chew. Then she looked at Derek—really looked, in a way that felt like taking inventory. At the protein bar he was still holding without offering. At the crowbar he was gripping like a security blanket. At the way he'd positioned himself between her and the exit.
"Can I—" she started.
"Elena." Derek's voice was a wire pulled taut. "We don't need their handouts."
"I haven't eaten in two days, Derek."
"I've been *saving* the protein bar. For both of us. For when we really need—"
"When do we 'really need' it?" The words came out cracked, exhausted, pushed past a breaking point that had been thinning for days. "When we're dead? When you've used me as bait one more time?"
The temperature in the garage dropped. Derek's face went very still.
"What did you say?"
"You heard me." Elena's voice shook, but she didn't back down. "Last night. When the zombies came to the ramp. You grabbed my arm and *pushed me toward them* so you could run back inside. You—"
"I was *saving us*—"

"You were saving *yourself*." She was crying now—silent tears tracking through the grime on her face. "Just like you saved yourself when Gary got attacked. You pushed Gary into the horde and you ran. I saw you. I *saw* you, Derek."
The name *Gary* detonated in the garage like a bomb.

**[HIDDEN INTEL EXPOSED: Derek Cole previously sacrificed a companion (Gary) to zombies to save himself]**
**[This is the dark secret referenced in Quest T005]**
**[Thread T005: Derek's Dark Secret — NOW ADVANCING]**
Derek's mask cracked. Underneath was something ugly—not shame, not guilt, but *fury*. The fury of a man whose carefully constructed narrative was being dismantled in front of witnesses.

"You don't know what you're talking about," he said, but his voice had climbed an octave, and his eyes had gone to Sera, to Marcus, to the exit, calculating escape routes with the desperate cunning of a cornered animal.
"I know you pushed Gary." Elena wiped her face. "I know you've been rationing food for yourself. I know you have something on your shoulder you won't let me look at."
The crowbar came up.

Marcus didn't see the transition—one moment Derek was standing, the next he was lunging, two steps toward Elena, crowbar raised, face twisted into something that wasn't entirely human anymore. His pupils had blown wide—not stress response, something else—and the veins on his neck were dark and visible, pulsing with something that wasn't quite blood.
Sera moved.

She was there before Derek's swing completed—faster than thought, faster than the eye could track, her hand closing around the crowbar mid-arc and *stopping it dead*. The impact should have broken her grip. Instead, Derek's momentum halted like he'd hit a wall, and Sera plucked the crowbar from his hand like taking a toy from a toddler.
She tossed it over her shoulder. It clattered into the darkness.
"Touch her again," Sera said quietly, "and I'll remove your arm. Not as a threat. As a promise."

**[COMBAT AVERTED: Seraphina's intervention prevented physical harm to Elena]**
**[Derek's Infection Status: Stage 0.5 — Pre-turn aggression + loss of inhibition]**
**[Time until full turn: 24-36 hours]**

Derek stumbled back, cradling his hand where Sera's grip had left bruises. His face was a war between terror and rage—and the rage was winning, feeding on whatever the virus was doing to his brain chemistry.
"You're dead," he snarled at Marcus. "You and your zombie whore. I'll—"

"You'll do nothing." Marcus's voice came out steadier than he expected. The Survivor's Edge jacket felt armor-thick around his shoulders, and Sera's presence at his flank was a wall of cold certainty. "You're infected, Derek. I can smell it on you. That scratch on your shoulder? It's changing you. Making you more aggressive. Making you *less*. In a day, maybe two, you won't be Derek anymore. You'll be one of them."
Derek's hand went to his left shoulder. An unconscious tell. "You're lying."
"Take off your jacket. Show Elena."
Silence.
"Show her, Derek."
Derek's jaw worked. His eyes flicked between Marcus, Sera, and Elena—three people who were no longer afraid of him, and the absence of their fear was a void he couldn't fill with violence anymore.
He ran.
Not toward them—toward the ramp, away from the garage, into the grey morning light of the dead city. His footsteps echoed, then faded, then were gone.
Elena stared after him. Her face was a battlefield—relief warring with guilt, liberation warring with loss.
"He's going to turn," she said. "He's going to turn, and he's going to be out there, and—"
"And we'll deal with it when it happens." Marcus held out the container of salmon. "But right now, you need to eat."
She took the container. Her hands trembled. She sat on the floor—just sat, right there on the oil-stained concrete, and ate like she'd forgotten food could taste like anything other than survival.

**[QUEST UPDATE: The Knight in Rusted Armor]**
**[Progress: 60% — Elena is no longer under Derek's direct control]**
**[Remaining Objectives:]**
**[- Secure Elena's voluntary agreement to relocate to your safehouse]**
**[- Complete the Conquest Quest milestone: Hold Elena's hand for 3 minutes in Derek's presence]**
**[Note: Derek has fled but is not gone. He will return—likely during his final human hours.]**
Sera crouched beside Elena, keeping a respectful distance. "The food isn't poisoned. I promise."
"I know." Elena took another bite. Chewing slowly. Tasting. "You could have killed him. You didn't. You didn't have to be merciful, but you were."
"Marcus asked me to be."
"Marcus asked." Elena's eyes found his across the garage. "Why?"
Because the System told me to? Because there's a quest? Because—
No. Because it was right.
"Because no one should have to choose between one monster and another," Marcus said. "You deserve better than the worst option."
Elena held his gaze. Something shifted in her expression—the last wall coming down, the final calculation resolving.
"When can I move?"
